OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
    "mode":"human",
    "topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
    "opinion":"I've seen plenty of misinformation online, even though I'm not glued to my phone all day like some people. As a carpenter in Naples, Italy, I mostly use social media for quick research or staying connected with family when I have the time, but occasionally I come across fake stuff that really bothers me because it can feel so convincing and sometimes leads me down the wrong path.

One thing that's worked well for me personally is to question everything—especially if something seems too good to be true or makes a bold claim without any proof. Since my depression means I'm already more sensitive than most, encountering fake content about health scares or conspiracy theories can really amplify those feelings and make it harder to focus on real tasks like building furniture.

I try not to worry too much about the big picture stuff online because my education is only up to upper secondary level; sometimes complex topics just confuse me more when they're misrepresented. Plus, having grown up in Naples with no formal schooling for my dad means I learned early that facts matter, and trust comes from being able to verify something rather than accepting it blindly.

My low openness score actually helps here—I'm not easily swept away by new ideas or trends online without evidence, so fake content feels more like an annoyance than exciting news. And because of my high conscientiousness, I appreciate clear information that allows me to make informed decisions without getting caught up in drama.",
    "reasoning_summary":"Step 1: Analyze the persona traits (high conscientiousness, low openness) and how they influence approach to truthfulness online.

Step 2: Consider practical limitations based on occupation (carpenter) and time spent on social media (few times weekly).

Step 3: Address psychological factors (depression) affecting vulnerability to misinformation.",
    "tone":"Pragmatic, grounded in reality",
    "citations":[],
    "safety_notes":"None needed as persona does not generate or consume harmful content.",

}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
I've seen plenty of misinformation online, even though I'm not glued to my phone all day like some people. As a carpenter in Naples, Italy, I mostly use social media for quick research or staying connected with family when I have the time, but occasionally I come across fake stuff that really bothers me because it can feel so convincing and sometimes leads me down the wrong path.

One thing that's worked well for me personally is to question everything—especially if something seems too good to be true or makes a bold claim without any proof. Since my depression means I'm already more sensitive than most, encountering fake content about health scares or conspiracy theories can really amplify those feelings and make it harder to focus on real tasks like building furniture.

I try not to worry too much about the big picture stuff online because my education is only up to upper secondary level; sometimes complex topics just confuse me more when they're misrepresented. Plus, having grown up in Naples with no formal schooling for my dad means I learned early that facts matter, and trust comes from being able to verify something rather than accepting it blindly.

My low openness score actually helps here—I'm not easily swept away by new ideas or trends online without evidence, so fake content feels more like an annoyance than exciting news. And because of my high conscientiousness, I appreciate clear information that allows me to make informed decisions without getting caught up in drama.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
Step 1: Analyze the persona traits (high conscientiousness, low openness) and how they influence approach to truthfulness online.

Step 2: Consider practical limitations based on occupation (carpenter) and time spent on social media (few times weekly).

Step 3: Address psychological factors (depression) affecting vulnerability to misinformation.
response_parsed.safety_notes: None needed as persona does not generate or consume harmful content.
